Ahoi liebe Leute, 

Salt and Sacrifice ist ein Spaßiges und Gelungenes Spiel im Salt and Sanctuary Universum. 
Es grenzt sich allerdings mit anderen Gameplay von Salt and Sanctuary ab und man sollte es nicht als Nachfolger betrachten. Das ist es nämlich nicht sondern ein Spinoff. 

Die schlechten Bewertungen rühren auch daher das diese Leute ein Salt and Sanctuary 2 erwartet haben, was man aber nicht bekommt und daher die Enttäuschung für sie Darstellt. 
Kann ich verstehen wird dennoch den Game nicht gerecht, mich haben die vielen Schlechten Bewertungen sogar verunsichert. 
Ich bin allerdings sehr froh das ich den Game eine Chance gegeben habe weil mir Gefällt es. 

Aber wenn es kein Salt and Sanctuary 2 ist, was ist es dann ? 
Am Besten lässt es sich als ein Crossover aus Salt and Sanctuary und Monster Hunter umschreiben.

Wir sind ein Inquisitor der dazu verdammt ist, ein von Bösen Magiern heimgesuchtes Land von diesen zu befreien. Wir besuchen einzelne in sich geschlossenen Karten und jagen da unterschiedliche Magier über die Karte bis wir sie in ein Finalen Kampf bekämpfen und schlagen.
Die Geschlagenen Magier und ihre Minions lassen Ressourcen fallen die wir zum craften neuer Ausrüstung nutzen können. Der Grind dabei ist nicht so Heftig wie in Monster Hunter. 

Jeder einzeln dieser Magier wir eingeführt durch eine Jagd auf einen "Namenhaften Magier" haben wir diesen geschlagen so treffen wir die "Namenlosen Magier" an, diese können im Grunde das selbe wie ihre Namenhaften Kollegen dienen aber zum weiteren Farmen von Ressourcen. 
Neben den Magiern gibt es auf jeder Karte auch noch weitere Bosskämpfe gegen diverse Zwischen-Bosse.

So prügeln wir uns durch die einzelnen Level und schalten Stück für Stück weitere Bereiche dieser Orte frei oder finden Runen für das Freischalten weitere Level. 
Starten tut unsere Reise immer von einer Hub wo wir diverse Händler und anderer NPCs antreffen, ähnlich wie Majula in Dark Souls 2. Über ein Portal mit Runen "Wählscheibe" reisen wir in die anderen Level.
Man kehrt auch immer wieder in die einzeln Level zurück sobald man neue Fähigkeiten für die weitere Erkundung freigeschaltet hat oder falls man bestimmt Ressourcen sucht. 
Mir gefällt dieser Gameplay Loop und besonders der unkomplizierte "Drag and Drop" Multiplayer sagt mir sehr zu. Dieser ermöglicht es uns, falls wir mal ein Boss nicht schaffen, andere Mitspieler für diesen Kampf anzuwerben. Diese bekommen dann auch etwas von der Beute, zudem kann man auch ähnlich wie in Dark Souls geraidet werden, was aber nur passiert wenn wir einen Mitspieler bei uns haben. 
Man kann also auch beruhigt im Singelplayer Leveln und Looten ^^

Ebenfalls gefällt mir Besonders der Art - Stil : Rüstungen und Gegner/Bosse schauen Prima aus und man hat für jeden Geschmack etwas. 
Es gibt Schwere und Leichte Rüstungen, diverse Waffen Gattungen und ein Elemente System was eine nette "Stein, Schere und Papier" Dynamik ins Spiel bringt. 
Unserer Gegner haben verschiedenen Element Typen und diese gilt es mit den Passenden Loadout zu kontern um uns den Kampf zu erleichtern. 
Nach meinen Empfinden ist es ein wenig Einsteiger Freundlicher und "Leichter" als Salt and Sanctuary, was aber nicht heißt das es einfach ist. 
Zusätzlich sollte man erwähnen das es keine Karte gibt, was ein Hindernis sein kann da die Levels teils ganz schön verschachtelt sind. Dies tut aber auch kein Abbruch und reizt zumindest bei mir den Entdecker Drang. 

Kauft euch das Spiel nicht wenn ihr Salt and Sanctuary 2 erwartet.
Aber wenn ihr diese Universum liebt so wie ich und offen für etwas neues seid, dann solltet ihr den Game eine Chance geben.

Soviel zu meinen Eindruck zu Salt and Sacrifice !

只想過一個結局，遊戲即便過了一年還是能感覺到黑暗靈魂2的疊怪跟惡意，簡單的說，玩家處於魂系的遊戲中，小怪跟王不是在無雙就是在格鬥遊戲連段中，而且真的追王追得非常疲倦。

Since the epic games launch this game has substantially improved in a lot but it still suffers from an identity crisis. 
It's a fusion of a Souls like and a Monster hunter like title ; in itself thats not the worst thing, but while trying to do both things equally it makes for an obtuse experience.

Mages attack patterns seem wildly inconsistent with very high damage and knockback attached where even the toughest armor still sees you losing half your health to something the mage easily filled the screen with and is still doing. Due to the gameplay loop being following mages across awkwardly small and perilous stages until you arrive at a small arena to be quickly filled with attacks , projectiles, roll catches-you name it. All mages feel about the same with little variety, which is a shame and a let down from both angles of souls like and MH-like.

Chase sequence take you through Souls-like maps which means filled with traps, lethal falls and high damage mobs; in later areas some mages will run away past a jump puzzle and once you reach it; it will run away back through it. When youre not dealing with hunting a mage, the maps themselves are decent the only issue with them is due to the comically strong knock back a lot of things have , youre often having to do that climb you just did all over again.

Oddly enough the most well designed fights that make for the better experience are not the mages, but the other non mage bosses such as the dragon. Having a dedicated stage thats spacious or having attack patterns that are telegraphed and have space between themselves to breath makes for a fun experience- who wouldve guessed.

Edit-The Final boss did play a different track this time? I had gone back to help a friend for the other ending and this time it wasnt the default mage theme. Probably a bug; leaving that bit in just incase someone else runs into it
Music is practically nonexistent ; Mages have 1 shared track which the final boss also uses.. Other bosses also have one shared track, most of the time you spend your time in awkward silence or very faint ambiance. Exceptions are the final map and a section of the new one they added in do have dedicated tracks but its strange seeing such a drastic drop in variety from the maker of Dishwasher series or Charlie murder. Its understandable wanting a more unsettling ambiance focused track for the tone and setting of the game- Monster Hunter World did not have constant tracks playing for every map for example; but that did have different battle themes for each map for small and big enemies alike- as well as a short intro theme for day and night- i had wished something similar was implemented as a break away from the unnerving silence.

I want to love this game, SKA and specifically James Silva has made my favorite games and favorite sound tracks , and maybe this didnt have to be Salt and Sanctuary 2 or even similar; its own thing as he clearly wanted it to be- but i do wish It leaned more in one direction or the other. Trying to do both as is just doesnt work for me; unfortunate as i love both genres but i cant recommend it. Do try SKAs other games though its predecessor Salt and Sanctuary i cannot recommend enough, and Dishwasher vampire smile has a killer sound track.

「建議喜愛一代的玩家購買前三思」
「完全把二代當成新遊戲來玩比較能夠接受」

製作組忘了當時一代是如何廣受玩家好評的，
就是大量「致敬」黑魂而分到魂遊戲市場的一杯羹，
而如今抄襲抄出優越感，竟然連魔物獵人都想抄，
就誕生了二代這個畫虎不成反類犬的畸形之作。

進入遊戲不久就會產生一個最大的疑問：「BOSS到底跑夠了沒」
本作為了迎合「狩獵法師」的主題，讓BOSS在地圖不斷跑動，
不只地圖怪物全部復活，還要對付新召喚的中立怪物，
大量堆怪讓追逐過程非常煩躁，浪費相當多時間在處理小怪。

製作組似乎還喜歡在地圖邊緣擺有「擊飛」技的小怪，
跑到懸崖邊常常被小怪擊落從頭再跑一次地圖，
此外小怪的「擒拿」技能太多，動畫時間又長，
逼著玩家看自己角色被怪物蹂躪又無力回天，
真不懂這樣搞玩家有何樂趣可言。

BOSS戰就能感受到製作組不夠用心在打磨遊戲，
戰鬥過程就像玩家是用50%的速度，而BOSS則是200%，
玩家喝水速度過慢、翻滾後動畫時間長，不對等的速度、
加上BOSS過快的攻擊頻率，導致沒有空檔只能單方面挨打。

相信製作組在新的一代希望能走出新的路線，
但大破大立之下卻沒設計出合理的遊戲機制，
會購買本作的很多都是看在一代的感動而入坑，
但本作卻只能說是背棄了上一代玩家的感動。

雖然大多為批評，但我在遊玩過程還是獲得不少樂趣，
洋洋灑灑寫了這麼多就是對製作組仍然抱有期待，
希望製作組不要灰心喪志，如果還有下一代能回歸初心，重拾的玩家的口碑。

盐与献祭成功展示了什么叫做野心有余能力不足。制作组知道大家很喜欢打盐与避难所的大湖女巫这场boss战，所以贴心的为大家制作了二十余个大湖女巫一样弹幕满天飞的法师供大家反复打和刷，并且为了防止大家在跑图过程中感到无聊，贴心准备了一大批野生法师乱入，每次进入地图打法师都是整个晋西北乱成一锅粥，混乱度堪比雨世界的高难度生态。可以看出本作的内容量非常庞大，每种法师都会召唤数种不同小怪，光20多种法师对应的小怪数量就非常庞大，同时还有更多武器种类对应的动作模组和战技模组，可以想到制作这些内容的工作量有多庞大。于是顺理成章的，本作在其他方面几乎全面倒退于盐与避难所，动作系统、地图设计、关卡设计、多周目数值，除去从盐与避难所祖传的问题之外剩下的全是退步，同时各方面打磨十分粗糙。可以看出，本作堆料很足，但设计方向错了堆料再多也没用，这是大家能从盐与献祭得到的教训。只能说经过制作组一年多的修改之后游玩体验尚可，在刷法师的时候野生法师乱入也能提供一些雨世界那样电子斗蛐蛐的乐子，不过远称不上优秀，至少在盐与避难所续作这个名号下完全不及格。

I have played this game for quite a few hours looking for an excuse to write a positive review. Sadly, Salt and Sacrifice is not a good game. If I wanted to compare it to Salt and Sanctuary, its predecessor, they wouldn’t even be in the same league. So I’m going to try to explain why this game is mediocre at best. It’s a metroidvania trying to be Monster Hunter, but it falls short. Boss fights are nothing more than a sad exercise in button mashing without much strategy, and almost all fights feel the same with little variation. The maps have a lot of verticality, which would make it interesting that you have to chase bosses to hunt them down, if it weren’t for the fact that absolutely any enemy (boss or mob) can send you flying with a simple attack and send you back in a rather frustrating way, if the fall doesn’t kill you. That is, if you haven’t found a trap in the middle of the map, which is there only to troll the player. One of the common mistakes that these types of games have is that they boast of having great difficulty when the reality is that their difficulty does not lie in their mechanics, but in the statistics of the enemies, which is quite frustrating. When I finished the game, I thought that a new run in NG+ mode would give me a new perspective, since the first run was too easy for me. However, I found that the enemies scale in power much more than my character can. This makes boss fights impossible in some cases where two or more bosses are in one part of the map and, in addition to the enemies that each one summons, there are also enemies from the map itself. If I can’t even see what’s happening on my screen, it’s not difficult, it’s unfair.

Salt and Sacrifice tries to be something new, but falls short and fails to shine in any of its aspects. The combat is decent, but there is nothing better than in Salt and Sanctuary. The lore, as always in these games, is cryptic enough that I didn’t even understand the story well, and it invites little exploration. The level design is decent. Overall, it’s a game that scores 5-6 out of 10. And if compared to Salt and Sanctuary, it’s a “don’t waste your money” out of 10.

If you are looking to get this game because you liked Salt and Sanctuary, STOP AND READ first! This game is not like its predecessor. I'm currently 34 hours in and will only complete this game because I spent money on it. I don't find it fun or entertaining, just frustrating. So lets get into it.

Compared to Salt and Sanctuary:
It does not contain in interconnected world where you can move from on zone to another. It seems as though the developer abandoned their original ideas to follow those that are proven or expected to enhance game play. The sanctuary system is not in this game, preferring to use a dark souls 3 hub. It is the only place you can level, craft, enhance equipment, and move from one zone to another. There are NPC here, but outside of purchasing a few items they are almost entirely PVP based. Unlike the previous game NPC do not enhance, upgrade, or transmute gear. They literally included these NPC to talk to, dialog that is exhausted almost immediately. There are almost no eldritch horrors bosses to fight. The main point is to hunt down mages, that once defeated appear through out the zones mostly getting in your way. Which wouldn't be too bad if the only real way to gain experience wasn't gotten from them and them alone, mobs drop almost no EXP! Lastly, when compared, the interesting movement and and abilities from the previous game have been dropped in favor of those from other successful titles. Floating up wind currents, used in Ori, fail as they are never low enough and don't actually send you up is you have any horizontal momentum. Grappling hook, used in Halo, are basically uncontrollable and often send you flying where you are not trying to go. The grapple points are also often place horrifically. Magic is almost gone, this game uses weapon arts, like in Elden Ring.

As a stand alone game:
I think this game is just abysmal even when you don't compare it too Salt and Sanctuary. I would say that it can be described as either unrefined or rushed. Bosses are not really that unique, they all seem to fit in the exact same hit box with very generic differences, they all summon minions and their move sets are fairly easy to see. I think they were included in the world, outside boss arena, to solely pad out the game time. 34 hours is very long for a 2d game, especially when not fun, and I still haven't beat it. Control and movement wise, I use the same controller I use for every other game and this game just seems to have issues. It's not the very common occurrence of the game putting attacks exactly where you are dodging to, like input reading. Its not the dodge being too far to the point where it regularly puts you out of range to attack, especially when this game seem to seek to only ever give you enough time to strike once before you need to dodge again. The real issue is that it seems to loose button inputs. Need to grapple to avoid falling to you death, failed to accept button input. Trying to use a ranged weapon art requiring you to hold block then press Y, get interrupted and get locked into a stream of melee attacks that drain you stamina. Honestly, the thing that makes this game so frustrating to me is the damage sponge enemies, the utterly excessive traps with no visible triggers, and over reliance on enemies that that hid and pin you down to stab you to death.

The developers had such a good jump off point with Salt and Sanctuary, but for whatever reason abandoned it and ended with a game that was enticing but ultimately enjoyable.

Oh god where do I start.
Firstly, I know I haven't played this game much, and I'm going to keep that in mind while writing my review. 
Like so many others in this review section, I'm an ABSOLUTE fan of Salt and Sanctuary. I've poured 300 hours of my lifetime into it, tried out every possible build (except magic because... well.. It's magic), killed every boss, nuked every boss, gotten every achievement... I just couldn't get enough.

That being said, Salt and Sacrifice lost me after I killed the fourth electromancer in a row, and those aside, I think I've killed 4 unique bosses so far and all of them were just so forgettable.. I don't even remember their names... In comparism, I'm confident that everyone here who's played Salt and Sanctuary remembers The Sodden Knight.
Back to the mages... Why do I have to play cat and mouse with them? ._. - I get you wanted to make a mage hunter, but it just feels so unrewarding running through an area you've already finished to find a boss you've already beaten, especially since some of the areas are ABSOLUTELY not fit for boss fights.
 
Next point. Random enemy spawns... No... Learning fixed patterns and spawns and capitalizing on them is, imo, an essential part of any (FUN) souls like. And random spawns just make that impossible. I've seen a lot of people complain about repetitive enemies, but tbh I for my part haven't noticed it...
 
The combat actually feels smoother than S&S, I like the sprinting mechanic for example. It actually feels very fast paced, love it. I have one huge problem with the combat tho. No Iframes when you get knocked down is catastrophic. You can just get snowballed out of existance. I've died a couple times because an enemy launched me into the air and then homerunned me into oblivion.

Lastly, I gotta point out the obvious pros. I absolutely adore your graphic style. Please never change it. Period. 
The skill tree is as good as ever, Ive found some good weapons for my style fairly early on, and multiple for other builds had I wanted to pick smth other than pure strength :D. 
I also like the charm system, adds a layer of complexity to builds. 
The built in throwing weapon was kinda useless to me, thenagain I haven't found any strong ones yet because I haven't played the game much. 
Lastly, the map designs are mostly good, very often built either linear or circular., I just felt like players who are new to no map games might get lost at one point or another. Thenagain that's just me assuming.

Believe me when I say that I give this thumbs down with a heavy heart. I've played Dishwasher and Salt and Sanctuary and I absolutely loved both of them, and it hurts me seeing this game fail the fanbase as much as it does. I just feel Salt and Sacrifice got lost in ideas... I hope you're not discouraged to keep going by this game flopping, you both have a gift for making games, and I'd love to see a real Salt and Sanctuary 2 someday. :3

A decent game that brings some elements from the previous Salt game, but has different gameplay loop which combines soulslike and monster hunter:
- character progress(soulslike leveling, but MH equipment crafting) 
- level structure (mostly soulslike with unlockable shortcuts and checkpoint) 
- boss fights (MH-esque where you chase a boss around the stage and end in a boss arena, but soulslike in that you have infinite retries on the fight and won't fail the hunt unless you chose to abandon) 
Which might lead to some frustration because while there is no fall damage in MH, fall damage is in full force here. 
Overall, I recommend this game so people will suffer with me, screw you Bol Gerahn

Most negative reviews come from one of two places.
1. Close-minded people expecting this game to be exactly like the first. (Like how people hate Dark Souls 2 not because it was bad, but because it was different, and not what was expected)
2. People who got frustrated at one specific inconvenience/occurrence. (Like, say, getting mad that enemies were knocking them off ledges)

Salt and Sanctuary was 2D Dark Souls. Salt and Sacrifice is 2D Dark Souls AND 2D Monster Hunter. The game's formula follows Monster Hunter more closely than Dark Souls, but players of both games will feel right at home here.

I highly encourage you to look past the mixed reviews and try the game out for yourself. The game's mechanics are very streamlined and simple compared to most other games of its mixed genres. Even if you've never played Dark Souls, Monster Hunter, or any of Ska Studios' other titles, you can get into this game and have a good time. Because it's not a sequel and is barely related to Salt and Sactuary story wise as well, the game's story will not leave you with questions only answerable by another game.

Get this game.

とても楽しんだソウルライクゲームだったので、続編をプレイすることを楽しみにしつつ、今回はレビュー評価が前作と比べて賛否両論になっていたので不安でしたが、一言で言うと自分にとっては「ちゃんとソルトの続編として楽しめるゲーム」でした！
（ちなみに自分は、ソウルライクゲームは好きだけどなんでも楽しめるわけではない＆モンハンはシステムが合わなくてほぼプレイしていない感じの傾向です）

評価を調べるとサムズダウンが目立つのかなぁと思うのですが、自分は十分楽しくプレイできたので「前作が好きだったけど、評価を見て迷っている人」の参考になれば幸いです。

[h3] ◆批判されている要素についての感想 [/h3]
自分の感想は以下です。
総じてそんなに気になりませんでした。

・ボスが逃げ回るシステムについて→賛否分かれる事になった大きな要素はこのシステムなのかな、と思うのですが、いうほどソルトの良さが削がれている感じはなく、確かに面倒に感じることもあるけど、ボスが複数湧くとお互いに戦い始めて怪獣大戦争みたいになるのが楽しかったし、個人的には良い面も悪い面もあるけど基本的には楽しめた、という感じでした。

・採集要素がめんどい→自分も、これは必要なシステムなのか？とは思いましたが、各地でこつこつ集めるのはストレスというほどではなく、採取場所があると少し楽しい気分になったので、なくてもいいけどあってもいい要素かなと思いました。ただ、この要素によりフラスコや矢弾が無限補給ではなくなっているので、枯渇する人はしんどいかもしれません。自分の場合は飛び道具主体の戦闘スタイルではなかったためか、クリアまでに枯渇することはなかったです。（あと枯渇するほどの進行度になれば銀も貯まっていて購入する事もできるので、言うほど不便ではないのでは、とも思いました）

・ボス周回がめんどい→確かに面倒な時もありますが、そんなにストレスに感じるほど無限に周回しないと強化できないという印象はありませんでした。ステージを探索していればしょっちゅう野良ボスに遭遇してそこそこ強化素材が手に入るので、足りないなと感じたらたまに周回ボスを呼んで強化素材を集めるくらいの気持ちでプレイしていました。

・レベル上げ場所が拠点にしかない→不便といえば不便だが慣れる。

・ステ振りがスキルツリーになった→自分はステ振りの方が好きなのですが、これはこれで楽しめました。尖った育て方をしにくいのが難点ですが、初心者にとっては楽なのかも？と感じました。

・罠がめんどい→見た目で分かるし覚えられる程度の数なので、自分は特に気にならなかった。

[h3] ◆個人的に楽しめた箇所 [/h3]
・全体的に楽しくプレイできました。前作で楽しいと感じていたアクションや戦闘の感覚は継承されており、ちゃんと続編として楽しむ事ができました。

[h3] ◆個人的に残念だった箇所 [/h3]

・前作の信仰システムが無くなった事でしょうか…。信仰システムはゲーム内でろくに説明もなく、分かりづらくはあったのですが、入信する宗派によってプレイングに影響が出るところが楽しく、所属意識も芽生えて楽しかったのですが、今作の信仰システムは完全にマルチプレイ用+装備品が買えるだけのシステムになっていたのが寂しかったです。

・ボスが基本的には魔道士なので、大きいくくりで「人型・でかい・同じ移動方法」なのでバリエーションに統一感が感じられてしまい、確かにボス戦は前作の方が楽しかったかな…と思いました。（でも普通に楽しめています）

[h3] 総括すると、今作も楽しくプレイできました！ [/h3]
次回作も楽しみにしています！

I'm gonna start with a big issue that also plagued me in bloodborne and demon's souls, which if you haven't played either is thus; you have to grind your healing item. The first Dark souls solved this issue which is why it baffles it was an issue in both this game and bloodborne, just have a set amount that gets replenished and have an item you get to increase your healing items. It isn't fun having to stop fighting a boss because I died too much and now I return to the very beginning because that's where these items are of course most plentiful. All it does it make me release a heavy sigh and think "not this again" 
Another issue; the character feels incredibly sluggish and unresponsive. Even with no armor, and a single weapon the character moves like an arthritic grandmother, and more than once I would roll, turn, and attack only to find that my character has chosen attacking the air next to hulking flame spewing goat headed creature is a better investment of time. 
The monster hunter influences would be tedious but luckily the free roaming versions of the bosses appear to have set spawn points because I could get each one to spawn at seemingly guaranteed locations, of course they would then teleport to somewhere I can't reach yet which caused another heavy sigh. 
All in all, can't recommend, combat doesn't feel fun just annoying, character moves like they're distracted by thoughts of doing anything else which makes them relatable I suppose, and as a last point having run attached to double tapping the wanted direction on the analog stick is stupid, just have me hold the dodge button.

所以其实这游戏游玩后半段我对远程的依赖越来越严重。可以说后期基本上没有远程赖不死的boss（吸血弩+血中铁），以及后面鬼畜的小怪也一样，近战反而变成辅助武器了，近战么可以也是可以，但是弄不好被击飞摔死，或是一两瓶血一只小怪（某些粪怪）。所以，谁还练近战翻滚啊，射爆就完事了。

dlc竞技场打两只法师的时候，有些时候为了躲技能，我经常会习惯性跑的远一点，但是游戏镜头却不跟过来，老是给法师特写，我人都出屏幕了画面都还在给法师镜头也是醉了，我因为这个死了很多次。

一周目还是可以玩玩的，近战吃力就远程，就这样

如果你是玩过盐与避难所的玩家，那么本作绝对值的体验。如果你是想玩横板魂like的玩家，那么本作恐怕不会太符合你的预期。

实际上相比于魂，本作的流程思路更接近于魂，血缘，以及怪物猎人的复合品。而在战斗思路上，你如果按一代的思路来会吃大亏。

本作难吗？很难。但是难到没有对策可言吗？不是。

由于同场多boss，多势力敌人以及复杂的环境陷进，本作更适合以游走寻找机会而非一个一个解决敌人来处理战斗。善用地形和敌人互相间的仇恨。

和魂不同，本作的远程武器是要远远强于近战武器的，而且由于弹药补充机制，你是可以把远程武器当作主战武器而不仅仅是辅助武器的。所以优先升远程武器吧。

由于架势条的引入以及没有盾，本作的几乎所有近战武器都有可堪一用的防御性能。所以大胆防御吧。

本作除了盐以外还有金钱作为货币，钱也会随死亡丢失，所以记得在出发前找商人把所有钱消费掉，推荐用来买血材料和弹药材料。

本作的法师狩猎，简单地说就是一个个小场景战，造成一定伤害后切换到下一个场景，直到显血条决战。在场景战中造成的伤害会影响最后的初始血量。在场景和场景之间，你可以坐火，但是不能回家。而且死亡不会重置进程。所以耐心的步步为营，你会发现其实要比直接开打更简单。

理解了本作的思路后，相信你可以发现本作的独特魅力。而这种体验是在所有魂like中独一无二的。

22 hours in now and pretty much everything about this game feels great! The only thing I've noticed is a little bit of a lack of non-boss enemy variety in some places.... people are mostly concerned about Mages being the only thing you fight though... Nah, those are recurring bosses you hunt down for items and story progression throughout the course of the game, THEY ARE NOT THE ONLY BOSS ENEMIES IN THE GAME THOUGH.
 There's still plenty of unique non-boss enemies to encounter too, like these freaks that run at you on all fours if you get too far away from them, they'll pin you down if they catch you and repeatedly stab you. 
All in all... I'm quite pleased by the gameplay, enemies, and all the hidden goodies I've been finding while exploring, I even fought some optional non-mage bosses last night that gave me a pretty good whipping before I beat them. 

 But yeah... I think alot of the hate may come from people who are upset that whatever cheesy tactic they used in the first game doesn't work in this one. That or they go in already poisoned by the toxic "this game sucks" sheeple mindset and only play the first area a bit--maybe a little bit of the second--see everything in a negative light, and proceed to parrot what they heard to everyone else. STOP DOING THAT!! 

 Another complaint I see is that the multiplayer community is too small or inactive... hilariously/sadly enough these complaints oftentime come from the same people who dump on the game and say how bad it is. No... If you want more players then stop telling everyone the game is bad. ESPECIALLY if you're still trying to play it. On that note, I would like to mention the pretty cool multiplayer options the game has, I've done a little bit of each so far and I can say I'm pleased with it.

> First off you have the classic Salt option: Co-op. Great experience! Vastly improved from the first game in that both players can get unique loot and progress their stories, special NPCs won't dismissively one-line you and not give you the pleasure of a full conversation. You can't invade or join other players' worlds in co-op mode, but you can still be invaded or joined yourself. Pretty cool.

> Next I'd like to mention the very souls-like "aid another player" and "ask for aid" options, they work great! You got candle things you can light when you are seeking to help somebody or looking for help and its pretty smooth sailing so long as whoever you connect to doesn't have a potato for a modem. There's also gestures available too! These are great, I always do a handshake and or a wave to somebody when I see them.

> Then you got the other souls-like "hunt a player" option. Same thing pretty much, you can light a candle saying you are seeking a challenger, or you can drop a cursed page to look for someone to invade. You meet, you fight, someone emerges victorious, bam.

> The last one is a very interesting one and probably my favorite: you have the runners and the sheriffs... Basically a game of cops and robbers; Runners invade other players' worlds to steal salt (exp) from the special enemies that spawn during mage hunts, in doing so they spawn dangerous enemies that attack the host player. Gather 9 times or kill the host and you win! If the mage hunt finishes before you do so however, you fail your mission. The sheriff players on the other hand are dedicated to entering worlds being pillaged by runners to hunt them down. Kill the runner to win!

> It is also worth noting that when you are in another player's world you have an indicator that regularly goes off that will show you the direction of your objective. If you're helping or trying to kill a player, the indicator shows you the direction of the host. If you're running, it will show you the direction of the sheriff, and the sheriff's indicator shows you the direction of the runner.

I definitely recommend this game to anybody who is a fan of the original and soulslike games in general. Its a shame the game released on Epic first... But now that its here on Steam it finally feels like its where it belongs. I look forward to an eventual third installment of the series if the hackjobs don't ruin its rep.

As someone who never played the previous game and was looking for a coop game, this one isn't a recommend. Mostly because the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ enemy random enemy placement that chases you till the end of the world, while also juggling you off the cliff with the occasional boss fight (that later turned into regular mobs) are just frustrating. The coop works fine, almost like Nioh and some part are even kinder, which is better than Dark Soul coop. It's just that most bosses do the same attacks (there are around 5 areas with each having 6 bosses, 5 of those are not unique and are just different elements), the armor that you get only have number difference instead of unique skill, and the enemies will juggle you a lot.

Bosses also have attacks that are too fast with spammable AoE, making heavy weapon almost useless due to its slow attack speed. I was able to play one because my other friend plays a light armor, so that if a boss attacks too fast they would be the one who carried the boss. Even with the balance update (that made the game from frustrating to tolerable, it's just not that good).

My suggestion for the dev, stop any development on here or finish up quickly and just focus on the next sequel (or game). Also, don't copy other games without thinking "oh, what does this do to our player". Dark souls game is a very flawed games that have been reviewed by many people from a rose tinted glass. We have been getting so many souls like these past few years that another indie soulslike/metroidvania would not be a great choice for your next game.

I have played this game on Epic because I liked it. 
Bought it again here so I can enjoy it on the go with the Steam Deck (and I just simply prefer Steam)

It is not like Salt&Sanctuary, which seems the point most people complain about. It has a monster hunter style "mage hunt" system, which can get tedious/boring if you play it for too long, in my opinion. 
For me it is a game I play for maybe one or two hours at a time, then take a break. 

The graphics are a clear upgrade to Salt&Sanctuary, the animations are good, you can pet the animals in camp. It looks lovely. 

The latest big patch changed a few things, how you lose salt/silver when dying and how you can get juggled by the enemies, it was overall a very good patch and addressed quite a lot issues. 

Overall I do recommend this game, but don't compare it to Salt & Sanctuary, because it feels nothing alike aside from the devloper and the art style.

Честно говоря, я разочарован. Я считал Salt and Sanctuary одной из своих любимых игры, а вот Salt and Sacrifice ощущается не как сиквел, который доработал и без того мелкие огрехи оригинала, а как двухколёсный велосипед, к которому с одной стороны приделали третье колесо. От трактора.

Казалось бы, это вся та же SaS, но нет. Боёвка сильно урезана - вместо абсолютно свободного выбора билда - у нас строгие наборы оружия, которые нельзя сменить (т.е. или два кинжала, или меч со щитом. Кинжал с щитом взять нельзя и т.д.). Кроме того, мувсеты абсолютно одинаковые, но добавили активируемые абилки на манер Elden Ring. Вариаций врагов стало будто бы меньше. Но, зато они научились прыгать через пол-локации и убежать от них в разы сложнее.
Ролевая система особо не поменялось. Дерево навыков выглядит красивее, да и какая-никакая система диалогов появилась. Но толку от этого не много. 
Боссы...ух, никогда не думал, что ОНИ смогут запороть ИХ. В SaN каждый из них был уникален, сложен и интересен. Здесь же - половина боссов (на текущий момент прохождения) или однотипные маги с небольшими вариациями заклинаний, или какие НЁХ, в которых ничего нельзя разобрать. Так ещё и некоторых из них нужно преследовать по локации, что ещё сильнее делает процесс скучнее.
Элементы метроидвании так же стали хуже. Вместо классических, но чрезвычайно полезных в бою рывков, двойных прыжков и отскоков у нас есть инструменты в виде крюка-кошки, магнитного камня и т.д. Всё-бы ничего, но полезность у них околонулевая - они приносят разнообразия в геймплей.
Ах да. Тут есть Крафт. И это всё, что я могу о нём пока сказать.
Вместо полноценного открытого мира, где можно было попасть из пункта А в пункт Б через пункты ЁПРСТ, у нас несколько отдельных локаций (по ощущениям, опять-таки, меньше тех, что есть в оригинале), а так же хаб. 

В общем, игра-то может быть и не плохая, но определенно не то, чего ты ждёшь от долгожданного сиквела Salt and Sanctuary

Esta reseña la escrito con la honestidad de un Fan que se leyó todo el lore de la entrega anterior, Salt & Sanctuary, y que esperaba respuestas y no más preguntas, pero vaya, empiezo ya, porque, el hecho de que mi reseña sea positiva, no implica necesariamente que sea un 10/10 este juego. 

Comencemos, como dirían, por el principio: Si eres un fan acérrimo del primer juego, quiero que tengas en mente lo siguiente - Este juego en realidad es parte del mismo mundo, pero no tiene demasiada relación en el lore, al menos no tanta como se espera. En realidad, tiene esa frescura que ese espera de una nueva entrega, pero nunca se definiría ni como secuela ni como precuela (presumo yo, luego de jugarme un rato éste y leerme el lore de los puntos de avance), y por ende, se da el permiso de ser muy divergente en comparación del Santuario. 

Las más puras diferencias radican en cómo cuenta su historia a través del juego mismo; Acá es más de cazar magos y ocasionalmente enfrentarte a un boss especial o principal, y con las piezas de los magos y sus guardianes, obtener mejores armaduras, algo así como el sistema de alquimistas de Sanctuary, pero en ésta siempre se tiene qué regresar al campamento a buscar al Corruptor en vez de tener ese sistema en un santuario con ofrenda en específico, lo cual puede sonar ya chocante; Segundo, en el primer juego, si bien la mayoría de las armas de alcance eran más de apoyo, verdaderamente eran basura con excepción de un par de arcos y la famosa Culebrina, mientras que acá tienen más sentido y mejor balance, aunque en ambos juegos, siguen siendo muy seleccionadas las armas que verdaderamente valen la pena; Tercero, precisamente con respecto a las armas, han aumentado en esta segunda entrega en muchas de ellas una segunda o hasta tercer escala sobre nuevos stats, que en un inicio no sólo confunden horrores, sino que además parecieran no repercutir mucho en un build o en varios intentos, es hasta que juegas o rejuegas el mismo que entiendes un poco la dinámica, el solo hecho de haber agregado un par más ya es muy confuso, eso sí, compensan "un poco" con ésto de los hechizos integrados a ciertas armas que permiten o usar el equivalente a bendiciones (Glifos benditos) o magia (Glifos prohibidos), que puede hacer un tanto más interesante la mecánica de ataque y combos. 

Ahora, la historia se centra en una tierra que, azotaba por corruptos magos sedientos de poder, desolaron todo ese mundillo, y tú eres parte de una orden de pecadores inquisidores que su condena fue volverse inmortales para servir y cazar para dichos magos y librar a Altarpetrea (o Altarstone, sea qué idioma estés usando) de tales males. Acá, como se ha dicho mucho, es cazar magos, es literalmente ir por todo el mapa cazándolos. Cuando inicias parece divertido que tengas que ir de mago en mago, obteniendo items y bla bla bla, pero conforme pasas o repasas un área (que por cierto, tienen muy limitado el viaje rápido), los atajos que desbloquees tienen un sentido y todo pero, incluso desde la primera partida, te puede caer la mala suerte de que te tengas qué enfrentar hasta a dos magos a la vez que no tienes por misión cazar, y puede ser o totalmente un auto-play para sacar corazones o puede ser un doble reto mientras aprender que el hacer parry pocas que ninguna vez van a ayudarte a hacerle esos bellos y sencillos counters que teníamos en la primera entrega. Sin embargo, solo es hasta el final que te revelan un poco de la historia de porqué ésto de cazar magos tiene algo de sentido, lo cual puede darle algo de directriz al juego y finalmente encontrar su belleza, luego de repetir a los mismos magos de diferentes niveles durante varios escenarios. 

Por último, el lore: Venga, la jugabilidad puede confundir porque esperas que sea otro Salt & Sanctuary, y en teoría lo es, pero tiene sus propias mecánicas para diferenciarlo de la primer entrega ¿Y qué diantres tiene qué decir la historia en su defensa? Bueno, esa creo que es la sorpresita. Fans de hueso colorado esperarían relación, respuestas, algo que te haga saber qué es el Dios sin Nombre, qué pasó entre la Guerra de Tristin y Askaria, qué pasó con los peregrinos que marcharon al Norte antes de llegar a Markdor, qué papel juegan la Diosa de la Luz Devara y su hermano oscuro Azredak, pero resulta que poco o nada de eso se te resuelve acá. De hecho, con forme lees la historia de la Expedición de Salt & Sacrifice, y con algo de ojo observador, te darás cuenta que es una historia quizás relacionada, pero completamente aparte, es quizás hasta otro continente, pues aquí no son ni humanos, ni lagartos, quizás sí gente de Jinderen, pero acá son "silvanos", algo parecido a elfos, y te podrás percatar por esa parte donde narran el encuentro con "gente de orejas curvas y redondas" y caes en cuenta de que tu personaje las tiene puntiagudas. Entonces, la historia de este juego se enfoca más en los poderes antiguos, en un intento por luchar contra fuerzas sobrenaturales, cómo en un intento por combatir un problema menor, puede que la Inquisición sea la causante directa de tanto mago que se dedica a cazar y la colateral decadencia de Altarpétrea, de narrar cómo esa tierra está condenada en un continuo ciclo y que probablemente este mal se extienda o ya se pasó a otras tierras, por lo que puede ser contemporáneo a Salt & Sanctuary sin ocuparse de la historia fuera de la Isla Desconocida. Mi teoría, para ir cerrando: El Sacrificio tiene un parentesco en diseño al Dios sin Nombre, por lo que ahí puede radicar su importancia. Eso ya lo veremos si sacan más actualizaciones o incluso una tercer entrega, que yo espero, retome más de la estética de Sal y Santuario que tanto encantó. 

En conclusión, es un juego bueno, disfrutable, pero siendo honestos, mucho más repetitivo que el primero, y que si no tienes paciencia (muuuuucha paciencia) y algo de curiosidad por el lore, te vas a sentir sumamente decepcionado, hastiado o demasiado ajeno a lo que hicieron en la primera obra, y puede que eso te haga percibirle como mal juego. Sin duda las mecánicas de pelea, especialmente para los parries es más de precisión y son poco dinámicos, además de que ahora la voluntad no mejora encuentro de objeto y resistencia, sino que ya son dos entes separados acá, y el que las Vanguardias ya no te permitan personalizar tu escudo y tu arma por separado puede hacerte tomar más tiempo en mejorar algunos aspectos, pero la gama y paleta de colores, diseños de personajes, estilos de armas y combates (mi favorita es la motosierra jajajajja spoilers), y sobre todo que dejan ver que el mundo de la Sal es más complejo de lo que inicialmente se pensaría hacen que sea un juego disfrutable tanto en multiplayer (razón principal del diseño de sus nuevas mecánicas y facciones) como en solitario.

Gotta say....with the latest patch the developer has really addressed a ton of the complaints I've had with my experience playing. The game still has some fundamental design issues that would make me hesitate to recommend this game to someone who usually doesn't go out for this genre. But if you're reading reviews for this game, you obviously know what you're getting into, and I can safely say that you'll get your money's worth.

ORIGINAL REVIEW

Super disappointing. Everyone said this is NOT Salt and Sanctuary II. Sadly, they were right. Game feels like it predates Salt and Sanctuary. Such a huge step backwards in so many areas. I really want to love this game. I loved Salt and Sanctuary. I want to love this developer. I want the developer to make more games in this genre. The developer clearly has a talent for 2d souls like games. But there are just so many confounding choices that were made in the development of Sacrifice. 

I want to chalk up the game's faults to a compressed development time and rush to release to meet Epic Game Store requirements. But the game has been post release for 1.5 years and it's just now hitting Steam in a state that feels shockingly identical to it's original release on EGS. Which is to say, this game has really lost the thread of what made it's predecessor so well loved.

Let me start with some things I like about this game. Love the look. Game looks great. Animations are on point, weapons look great, bosses look awesome, and the levels look good as well. Skill tree and character development are straightforward and easy for any new comer to get a handle on. The crafting system feels like a real hit. I think the dev really nailed the crafting system and I'd say it's absolutely an improvement over Sanctuary. Being able to craft multiple pieces from each boss by downing them more than once feels great. No more waiting till your new game plus to try a different weapon crafted from the same boss. Early game play feels great. There were a number of hours early on where I was like...I dunno what people are talking about, this feels like Sanctuary 2. Seems great. I definitely had a lot of fun starting out with this game.

Now to the problems....

The scaling in this game is just so far off the mark. There is a point in time before new game plus, where even basic enemies are deleting the player in a single short string of 2-3 hits. And I'm not talking light armor, 25% roll here...I'm talking full on upgraded heavy armor in every slot, soft capped 40 END and capped at either 50% or 75% roll. And it's not simply that Vitality is nearly worthless (despite running close to the soft cap). And it's not simply that even basic enemies do too much damage in a single basic quick strike (which they absolutely do). It's the fact that you combine this with the reality that even wimpy basic enemies have more poise to withstand interruption than a player in full heavy armor using a greathammer/greatsword/twohander. I cannot count the number of times, my 'quick' attack animation which started before the enemy's attack gets completely derailed and interrupted by their basic quick attack. Additionally more than half the time you actually do land damage first, they still manage to get their attack damage out, when conversely you do not in the same situation reversed. Fighting regular enemies in the game becomes a massive chore after the first 3 areas. It becomes so annoyingly time consuming and frustrating that there is simply ZERO reason to give a f*ck about actually playing the game, other than doing the bosses. Ok fine, so we'll just skip all the regular enemies because they're time consuming, frustrating to deal with, and worth virtually no salt to kill anyway. Oh wait....here we are, running through the rest of the game just trying to skip straight to the next boss. So if you like speed running, this game probably still has some value. But basically once you're halfway through the 4th area, everything just becomes a chore. The fun has all evaporated away. The bosses fights themselves feel like sh#%. The bosses in Sanctuary felt difficult but fair. And honestly I spent a lot more time wiping on them than I have in Sacrifice. But a number of the bosses in Sacrifice have just been a complete luck fest. It's simply a matter of attempts before the luck of the their attacks and placement allows you to get in the necessary number of hits. There are just too many bosses that create unavoidable damage situations for melee. It's like the dev just didn't want you to play melee. It's crazy. This game just BEGS to be cheesed. I've taken my heavy armor STR twohander/greatsword/greathammer build and had WAY better results just hitting bosses with throwing axes form distance. I wish it was a git gud situation. I really do. I know I could certainly improve. We all can. But even though I'm not spending as much time wiping to bosses in Sacrifice as I did in Sanctuary, they feel so much less satisfying to defeat. It's just a matter of so much luck involved in the difference between defeating a boss w/o getting hit once and getting put in a situation where this is no way to avoid taking a hit to get subsequently 100-0 w/o the capacity to recover. 

There are so many design decisions that really sap the fun out of the game and I think a lot of other reviews have really highlighted these issues. I don't want to belabor the negatives endlessly despite my desire to rant about all the frustratingly baffling choices that brought this game in it's current state to my desktop

I really wanted to make my first run though the game as melee which the general consensus was that it was significantly more challenging. Sadly it does not feel commensurately more satisfying to play. Conversely playing a ranged build allows you to avoid dealing with a lot of the design issues since cheesing mobs/bosses form distance makes them ridiculously easy.

The idea of Mage hunts was a really great idea. But wandering mages resetting on death just leads to massive frustration when so many of them can take you from 100-0 in a single attack or chain. It really is baffling. There is essentially one way to play this game. Skip regular mobs, go straight from one named mage hunt to the next named mage hunt. Skip everything else. Skip unnamed mage hunts, Skip wandering mages. Skip fated mage hunt. None of the activities in this game reward you for your time. Which would be find if the moment to moment game play were satisfying. So if you find it satisfying rolling past every normal mob, and shooting bosses form the other side of the map, boy is this game for you.

But if you want to play a melee build. And you want to fight your way through the game. And especially if you're deluded into thinking you'll have fun with 2handers....better check back later to see if the dev has come through with some changes. Or just wait for Salt and Sanity. I know this dev can put out a great game. Sanctuary was not a fluke. I'm going to choose to believe that getting into bed with EGS tied the devs hands somewhat and that next time around the dev will have the time and freedom to give us a game from the heart. Am I deluding myself in believing this? maybe. But despite my heavy disappointment in Sacrifice, I still hope this developer will continue making games in this genre. And I will hold out hope that Salt and Sacrilege will be even better than Salt and Sanctuary.

I overall enjoyed the game. I'm specifically putting this as "do not recommend" because the game instantly forces you into NG+ when you pick an ending. With new content specifically added with the steam release, which seems to be scaled for after the last area (at least partially), and a whole hidden area to explore, I expected that it would work like any of the souls games after the first one, where you got to choose when to go to NG+ after achieving an ending. But no, it just instantly resets your progress.

It's already too late for me to not feel bitter, but still, if they update it to not instantly force you into NG+ when beating the game, I'll change my review to recommended.
developer_response: We've recently made a change here where after beating the final boss you can walk away and come back later without the need to exit to the main menu. Also, the options to end the game give an indication it's a NG+ action. We're sorry for the frustration of unwillingly opting into NG+ when finishing the game before.

Con más de 55 horas y haber sacado el 100% de logros (Platino en PS) puedo hablar de la forma más coherente sobre este título y voy a dar mi opinión sincera.

Lo primero: 

¿Me gustó el videojuego? La respuesta rápida es SÍ.

Sin embargo quiero hacer una lista de las cosas que te vas a encontrar aquí y si esta "secuela" es superior al mítico Salt and Sanctuary que es uno de los mejores indies de los últimos años y quizás el mejor juego 2D inspirado en Dark Souls.

En resumen tenemos lo siguiente:

- El juego mantiene la misma estética que el S&S y mejora gráficamente.

- La jugabilidad es más agil y hay más movimientos en el gameplay lo que da un mejor dinamismo al combate.

- Hay muchas armas y tienes una grandísima cantidad de opciones para tu modo de juego.

- Desaparece el mapeado al estilo Castlevania para darnos mapas (muy grandes) con muchos secretos y con bastante Backtracking. Sin embargo, son pocos. Si, son grandes, pero son pocos.

- El sistema de bosses cambia radicalmente con respecto al S&S y además de tener a los bosses de la zona, tienes a los magos, que siguen siendo bosses, pero estos, tienen una afinidad específica a algún elemento y al ganarles, te sueltan los materiales con los que vas a craftear tus armas y equipo. Muy parecido al sistema de Monster Hunter.

- Se extrañan mucho las habilidades como el Doble salto o el Dash para movilizarte por las zonas. Tienes en su lugar el famoso gancho pero no es suficiente, solo puedes colgarte de puntos específicos del mapa y no es que sobren para movilizarte, sobre todo en aquellas fases del juego en las que estás en busca de ítems específicos.

En conclusión: Salt and Sacrifice como juego es muy bueno, te vas a entretener y lo vas a disfrutar. No obstante, en temas de dificultad está un par de peldaños por debajo de su precuela y la sensación de recompensa cuando derrotas a un Boss es inferior. Me explico, en Salt and Sanctuary, derrotar a un boss significaba el finalizar la zona y dar un paso a la siguiente. Aquí, como hay tantos bosses, derrotar a uno solo significa una gran recompensa de sal y probablemente no sea el único de la zona y tengas que seguir explorando.

Por otro lado, el diseño de bosses aquí y de las propias peleas es inferior al del anterior título. No me ha quedado ningún solo jefe en el recuerdo. En Salt & Sanctuary tienes al mítico Wyvern (que aquí tienes un homenaje a ese boss) del castillo, al árbol hijo de... al grifo, a la bruja del puente que hizo sacar canas a muchos y al Dios sin nombre que como batalla final era excelente, una batalla complicada y cuando al fin lo derrotas alzas los brazos en señal de victoria. Y pese que aquí, si, vas a celebrar contra unos bosses, el nivel de epicidad no es tan alto.

En cuanto a los mapas, como dije anteriormente, son muy grandes, pero la sensación de repetición está muy presente y en ocasiones te vas a aburrir. El sistema de mapas/zonas de Salt&Sanctuary era extraordinario, muchísimas zonas, no tan grandes, pero había muchísima variedad y el hacer backtracking era satisfactorio, aquí eso se pierde. Agregando también que el tema de los atajos aquí es muy inferior.

Salt and Sacrifice es muy buen RPG con inspiraciones a Monster Hunter y Dark Souls, gráficamente es superior a Salt and Sanctuary y la jugabilidad sigue siendo muy buena, aunque en mi caso prefiera al de su precuela. No obstante como producto final se queda por detrás del brillante título del 2016 porque éste, era excelente en mucho de sus apartados y aquí Sacrifice es muy bueno en unas cosas, pero en otras se queda a medias.

Al menos un par de veces al año me apetece volver al mundo de Salt and Sanctuary y aquí con Sacrifice creo que no va a pasar o quizás pase muchísimo tiempo para que lo vuelva a hacer.

Le doy un 7/10 mientras que al Salt & Sanctuary en su momento le di un 10 sin cuestionamiento alguno.

[h3] [url=https://ordenpixel.com/salt-and-sacrifice-analisis/] Análisis Completo en La Orden del Pixel [/url] [/h3]

Salt and Sacrifice nos lleva a las vastas tierras de Alterstone, un reino donde los criminales pueden optar entre dos vías de castigo: la muerte rápida o una vida de servicio eterno. No son pocos los que escogen la pena capital, aunque también están aquellos eligen servir. Estos parias de la sociedad quedan sentenciados a servir como Inquisidores Marcados tras someterse al «Rito del Martirio Mágico», teniendo como objetivo derrotar a una legión de magos que, desquiciados de poder, causan estragos en el mundo. La cacería no será fácil, puesto que tendremos que lidiar con bestias invocadas por los magos y con el propio terreno, lleno de puntos inaccesibles y trampas que causarán más de un problema en nuestra travesía.

La aventura inicia con nuestro protagonista reviviendo en el Valle del Absolutor, una suerte aldea poblada por comerciantes que nos proveerán de equipo y mercancías. A medida que avancemos en la historia comenzarán a aparecer no sólo nuevos diálogos, sino también equipamiento y habitantes con los que comerciar. En este mismo campamento es también donde podemos subir de nivel haciendo uso de un altar o acceder a las diferentes zonas del juego mediante un portal, utilizando una combinación específica de runas.

Al igual que en la primera parte, la moneda de cambio es la Sal, que también representa los puntos de experiencia obtenidos. Mediante el empleo de la misma, podremos subir el nivel del personaje que recibirá algunas mejoras mínimas en sus estadísticas. Gracias a ello, también obtendremos orbes que podremos invertir en un enorme árbol de atributos a través del cual desbloquearemos nodos para definir una clase con la que estemos a gusto. Salvo este último detalle, no hay demasiadas novedades en términos de progresión. Pero ojo, que ello no quiere decir que Salt and Sacrifice no tenga nuevas características.

Esta segunda entrega presenta una mecánica principal sobre la que gira la acción: la cacería de los jefes principales al mejor estilo de Monster Hunter. Nuestro personaje tiene un claro objetivo que es buscar y capturar a los dichosos magos. Al hacerlo recibiremos interesantes recompensas con las que podremos volvernos mucho más poderosos. Sin embargo, este sistema resulta ser bastante fastidioso. Y quizá se pregunten ‘¿Por qué?’. Principalmente se debe a que cada escenario tiene un puñado de magos sueltos rondando por ahí. Es decir, no se encuentran en un lugar específico, lo que nos obliga a explorar, lo cual está muy bien. El inconveniente radica en que al atacarlos, los magos buscarán huir teletransportándose a otra zona. Esto hace que el backtracking se sienta forzado puesto que estaremos obligados a recorrer áreas exploradas con anterioridad.

Es una suerte que, a pesar de lo mencionado, el juego goza de unos escenarios amplios y bien trabajados. Además, existe una suerte de progresión tipo metroidvania ya que podremos acceder a ciertas zonas luego de obtener determinadas herramientas. De este modo, conseguiremos desbloquear escaleras y puertas que funcionan como una suerte de ruta alternativa o atajo. También existen entradas protegidas por magia, las cuales sólo pueden atravesarse al derrotar un número concreto de Magos.

Por contraparte, hay una serie de elementos derivados de este aspecto que entorpecen el desarrollo de la aventura. Los escenarios son geniales y muy grandes, pero al no disponer de un mapa resulta muy fácil perderse o dar muchas vueltas. Conforme avancemos encontraremos checkpoints en forma de obeliscos que nos permitirán reabastecer los consumibles y recuperar vida. También marcan el punto de partida en caso de morir, aunque no funcionan para subir de nivel o realizar viajes rápidos. Sólo nos permiten volver al campamento principal, pero aunque ya se hayan descubierto, al regresar a la zona en que están ubicados, apareceremos en el principio del escenario. Por lo tanto, sí, nos veremos obligados a recorrer una vez más los mismos territorios.

Quizá pareciera que Salt and Sacrifice es una de cal y otra de arena. Pero la realidad es que se trata de un título bastante sólido a pesar de sus criterios de diseño. La jugabilidad es bastante ágil y ofrece un arsenal más que satisfactorio. La variedad de armas y elementos consumibles permiten adaptar nuestro estilo de juego a cualquier circunstancia. También disponemos de un sistema de crafteo que resulta esencial para mantener, mejorar y fabricar nuevo equipo. De hecho, es de este modo que podremos conseguir las mejores piezas de equipamiento del juego, fabricada a partir de la materia prima recogida de los entornos y de los restos de enemigos eliminados, o comerciando con los proveedores de cada facción.

Otra característica que suma mucho a la experiencia en general es el juego en cooperativo. Esta es otra de las grandes novedades que se incorporan respecto de la entrega anterior. Tal y como sucede en los juegos de la saga Souls, podremos solicitar asistencia a otros jugadores y trabajar en equipo, o invadir sus partidas y atacarlos. Las recompensas se reparten por igual, por lo que no tendremos que estar preocupados de que nos roben el botín. Eso sí, sólo podremos sumarnos a partidas de jugadores que estén en aquellas zonas descubiertas previamente, lo cual ayuda a que las partidas se mantengan niveladas.

Visualmente dista en cuanto a la paleta de colores aplicada en Salt and Sanctuary, cuyos entornos super apagados aportaban una sensación de opresión extremadamente oscura y tétrica, y nos sumía en un ambiente turbio que solo brindaba refugio a las atroces bestias sedientas de sangre. Para esta secuela, Ska Studios optó por una representación apenas más colorida y la utilización de un filtro que profiere un aura de reciente asolación del hábitat que se explora. La primera entrega daba a entender que las zonas ya se veían inmersas en una corrupción total. Un cambio que casi pasa desapercibido, pero que mantiene la esencia estética original pese a su nuevo tinte.

まだ途中ですが、とても面白いです。
前作も今作も2人協力プレイでコントローラーで遊んでいます。Remote play togetherです。
個人的にはソウル×ハクスラな感覚です。

◇ゲームの流れ
マップ探索→ボス撃破→集めた素材でクラフトや強化→再度マップ探索
同じマップでもストーリー進行やアイテムによって進める道が少しずつ解放されていきます。
すぐに先に進むことよりもマップをくまなく探索するのが好きな人向け…かな？
ストーリーは前作との繋がりはなさそうなので今作から始めても問題ないです。むしろ別作品的な位置づけなのかな。

◇難易度
最初からずっと2人プレイだからかほどよい難しさで楽しいです。
強化していくたびにちゃんと強くなっている気がするのでクラフトや強化のし甲斐があります。
ボスとの追いかけっこも道標を見失わない限りはそこまでストレスではないです。

◇微妙なところ
・拠点が広すぎる。特にマルチプレイだと別々の位置での作業がしづらいです。
・ボスの行方が分かりにくい。もうちょっと道標の表示を分かりやすくして欲しいです。(追記：仮面被った商人からボスサーチのアイテムを買えました！)
・アイテムソートの順番が謎。
・前作の方がボス戦は盛り上がったかな…。

◇他のレビューへの補足
・フレーバーテキスト→ちゃんとあります。
・拠点でしかレベル上げ等できない→難易度が上がってこれはこれで楽しい。
・罠→見れば設置場所はちゃんと分かるようになっているし数も多くない。

◇個人的総評
前作が非の打ち所がないほど素晴らしいゲームだったので、今作の実験感で賛否が分かれている印象です。
私はいまのところ十分楽しめていますが、行ったり来たりやマップ探索を好まない人には微妙かも。
マルチプレイでわーきゃー言いながら遊ぶのはとっても楽しいのでおすすめです！

The first time I played this game I absolutely hated it. Like a lot of people, I really really wanted and expected a similar experience to "Salt and Sanctuary". So going into this at first a lot of things were different/new/confusing and I ultimately didn't play it much after the release.

But after a few months I decided to play the game with fresh eyes & not compare it to their previous game- and so far I've already sunk over 10 hours simply because I love Jolly-CoOperarion. :praisesun:

Salt and Sacrifice seems to want to focus on an Dark Souls feel and overall Jolly Co-Operation and Invasion aspects of the game. If you've played anything made by FromSoftware you'd recognize a lot of things inspired from their games in S&S.

A main hub or "Firelink Shrine", a literal sunlight Covenant ("Affirm the Dawn" is such a cute reference to "Praise the Sun" 🥺), BOSS WEAPONS 🐦‍⬛📣, Solid Invasions only when you choose to summon friendly Phantoms- not to mention the naked red phantoms that can one-shot you.
I always get a chuckle out of the naked f*cks with a stick kicking my *ss, just like in the actual Dark Souls games. :praisesun:
I also always get a smile when myself and 2 others spam the block or jump button anytime we gank & kill an overpowered red.
A CAT YOU CAN PET
It's the little things in life lads.

I also really like that Salt & Sacrifice has Cross Play & an Password system so you can connect to friends easily. There's also a covenant item that makes area monsters attack red phantoms!

Overall I'm very happy with Salt & Sacrifice & I would happily recommend it to anyone who loves Souls-Like games.
BUT- If you have played Salt & Sanctuary before PLEASE don't expect Salt & Sacrifice to be similar to it. It's a completely different experience altogether but it's a happily welcomed one & I'm so happy that I gave it a second chance. 🖤
Far more of a Monster Hunter style game than a Dark Souls one, but still fun nonetheless.
Cult Was Sacrificed (for better graphics). Better played with Co-op. 8/10

Очень много изменений пр сравнению с первой частью и не все из них удачные.
- Теперь мир разделен на несколько больших локаций никак не связанных друг с другом и телепорт в которые осуществляется из хаба. Карты все еще нет, поэтому даже по уменьшенным локациям все равно тяжело ориентироваться и искать еше не открытые двери.
- Помимо боссов в игре есть схватки с магами, которые по сути тоже боссы, но до схватки на арене с ними их необходимо бить и искать на локации. Эти маги постоянно призывают всяких мобов из-за чего перемещение по локации становится тяжелым и хаотичным, а после победы над магом, он может появится в любой точке локации и продолжит спавнить поотивников. Поэтому он может помешать и в драке с другим магом. Да, есть в игре система противостояния магов, они могут бить друг друга, но все это превращает что исследование что сражения в такую мешанину и такой кошмар, что уже дальше исследовать локацию не хочется.
- С магов падают ресурсы из которых крафтятся доспехи, оружие, баффающие аксессуары, что интересно, но чтобы скрафтить все предметы необходимо гриндить этого мага и его приспешников.
- Деньги теперь есть куда тратить, по сравнению с первой частью, но они также пропадают после смерти и не подбираются, как например соль, что делает накопление на какие-то предметы невозможным
- Прокачка теперь только в хабе, и имея кучу соли приходится прерывать все исследование локации и тепаться туда. А я напомню, карты в игре нет.

[h1]По сравнению с первой игрой это определенно шаг назад.[/h1]

Окей я честно пытался полюбить сиквел, так же как смог полюбить первую часть. Но увы и ах, эта игра очень слабый проект.

Главная проблема этой игры геймплей. НЕТ, НЕ СЛОЖНОСТЬ. Сложность тут значительно ниже чем у первой соли. Очень много алтарей где можно восстанавливать ресурсы. Локации стали куда проще. Расходники тоже куда более доступные. НО. 

Алтарей много, но теперь на них нельзя прокачиваться. Извольте вернуться в хаб и прокачиваться там. Ах да вернуться в хаб можно с любого, НО между самими алтарями просто так перемещаться нельзя. Возвращаться можно только к особым. ГЕНИАЛЬНАЯ ИДЕЯ.

Локации стали куда проще, НО теперь каждая локация эта отдельная зона. Иии ещё разделенная дверьми с местным "гриндволом". Ой, чтобы пройти в эту часть локации нужно 1 сердце. Ой чтобы пройти в другую два, но уже других видов сердца. ЕЩЁ ОДНА ГЕНИАЛЬНАЯ ИДЕЯ, ведь сердца фармятся с местных "боссов". Иии ЭТО ЕЩЁ ОДНА ОЧЕРЕДНАЯ ГЕНИАЛЬНАЯ ИДЕЯ. Боссы фармятся на тех же самых обычных локациях. Дада, вам придется бегать по одним и тем же зонам чтобы фармить одних и тех же мобов. Потому что что? Правильно потому что с боссов и с того что они призывают падают РЕАГЕНТЫ ДЛЯ КРАФТА. Это какая уже гениальная идея по счету? Гении на разрабах очень сильно порезали дроп с обычных мобов по этому чтобы качать шмот проще убивать боссов. АХ ДА ОНИ МОГУТ ВПОЛНЕ СЕБЕ РЕСПАУНИТЬСЯ РЯДЫШКОМ. Два босса? ЧЕТЫРЕ СРАЗУ НА РЕСПАУНЕ - кушайте не подавитесь. АХ да, просто так этих клоунов тоже фармить нельзя - после нескольких тычек они меняют дислокацию и делают это несколько раз. Ура, ещё больше беготни по локации туда сюда и сражений с мобами + суммонами босса. Да эту игру делали сврехгении не иначе.

Добавим к этому полное отсутствие чего либо ценного в сундуках, бесполезных торговцев и лок нескольких видов оружия 5 тира за гриндом мультиплеерных активностей. Рецепт гениальной игры готов.

Окей хватит о грустном, ведь у игры есть плюсы: Крюк кошка - девайс который всегда дает пару баллов даже самой плохой игре(но не перзапуску DmC это недразумение даже крюк не спасает). Приятная графика - визуал стал чутка получше чем в святилищах. Да и в целом идея охоты на боссов не так плоха(жаль исполнение идеи дно).
Итог: Очень проходная игра которая уж очень сильно походи на длц. Хорошие идеи из первой игры выкинули, но ничего кардинально нового, на место удаленного, не привнесли. Баланс очень кривой, а скучного гринда стало в разы больше. Хотели сделать бладборн/секиру а сделали дарксоулс2. Увы рекомендую на ваше усмотрение. Основа крепкая, а детали не очень.

I'm enjoying the game, but I'm very much doing so in spite of its design, not because of it.

This is a metroidvania without a map. Intentionally so. And it doesn't do anything with that intention. You don't find a deeper appreciation for the game's mechanics because it's missing a map, and you don't engage with the game's world in an interesting way because it's missing a map. It's just missing a map, which sucks.

Worse, this is a metroidvania about backtracking. Hundreds of times, with default enemies that give basically zero exp, trying to remember which of the dozen identical doors just unlocked because you cleared a miniboss or which of the dozen identical heaps of rubbish still has ghost juice on it that you need to rub to fight the next miniboss.

70% of the game is tedious memorization and backtracking without a map. I'd love to know what the design rational was for this so I know what types of thinking to avoid.

So then, is combat better?

No.

It's weird to think about the Dishwasher games, because they had fluid, fun movement tech and were genuinely enjoyable to control. Then the developers made Charlie Murder and the controls were less fluid and more janky. Then Salt And Sacrifice, where the controls are janked to straight to hell. You do fortunately have some hyper armor when you've been knocked flying sometimes, which is good because even with an aggressive poise build most normal attacks from anything will send you flying. Enemy attack hitboxes are also the size of the moon and not clearly communicated by animations and *can* juggle you if they find their way past the hyper armor, which they do often. Your attacks, on the other hand, have hurtboxes smaller than their animations. No, I don't know why. Everything is scuffed, everything sucks, but combat does get a little more enjoyable when you play cheap.

Salt And Sacrifice is a game about using your tools to overcome crappy situations. The controls are a crappy situation, enemy hitboxes are a crappy situation, so you cheese ranged weapons and consumeables to supplement your melee attacks.

And there's even a difficulty curve to this. The better you get at cheesing the game, the better the game gets at making its hitboxes awful and limiting the fun you can have playing it.

For example, ranged attacks use consumable resources which you have to farm. Why? You already have a limited capacity you can carry without going through a lengthy crafting animation. You've already limited how much a player can spam them in combat. If they're meant to be powerful but scarce, why do you get one or two arrow crafting mats from every generic mob you fight?

The controls for firing ranged weapons also feel *awful*. I can't stress that enough. Hold LB + aim with right stick + fire with X. TF am I supposed to be pressing X with? My tongue? So you end up doing a *lot* more jumping neutral shots than aimed ones because even though gravity is cranked up 1000% on your projectiles and they immediately drop like a brick once they're out of your bow, you can kind of time them into an enemy that's walking towards you.

Despite, y'know, *all* of this, Salt And Sacrifice does have some cool elements too. I genuinely like the aesthetic and the setting, and the Monster Hunter style minibosses are a cool mechanical element. The skill tree is *alright* (though highly committal for reasons I don't understand,) and the parts of exploration where you're not tediously backtracking feel engaging. With some aggressive patching, this could be a game I'd recommend to any soulsvania fan.

As is, you need a pretty moderate tolerance for jank. If you have that, you'll have fun. If not, and if it hasn't been patched by whenever you're reading this (11.29.23 posting time), you're going to bounce off hard.

Edit: One last thing, which I'm including in case it helps someone out. The running kick is a better opener than any light attack. Also, light attacks are for stunlocking enemies that can be stunlocked. If you want quick, reliable combos with decent damage that you can tech roll out of in a pinch, you want the heavy attacks, which don't stun. No I have no idea why Salt And Sacrifice is like this.

2nd Edit: Rolled credits. The game was noticeably easier after discovering that your heavy attacks are for fast, non-stunning hits, and that your running attack should always, *always* be your opener for melee. That said, grab hitboxes are massively scuffed, hitboxes in general are scuffed, the game is just scuffed. And it looks nice enough that the stiff controls and weird hitboxes feel extra bad---everything that isn't the gameplay is pretty polished. My review is still a thumbs up, but barely. There's better janky soulsvanias. Probably get this one on sale.

Salt and Sacrifice is an amazing game. Ignore the negative reviews complaining that it’s not Salt and Sanctuary 2. It's a different game.

My wife and I were a little disappointed on our first playthrough on the PS4 because it was so different than Salt and Sanctuary, but it grows on you and we both now appreciate and love Salt and Sacrifice for what it is. After playing through both games as much as we have, we’re actually glad SKA Studios mixed up the formula a bit for this one.

Chasing Mages around the map may feel annoying at first, but once you start to know the maps and the Mages, know where they will go next, and what to expect when you fight them, it becomes a lot of fun. 

The level designs are a fun mix of platforming and enemy encounters just like Sanctuary. Because they were purposely built for chasing Mages around, navigating through them has a good flow to it and feels natural. 

I also really like that nearly every weapon has magic abilities. It makes each weapon feel unique and opens different strategies for combat. 

One issue we have with the game is how unbalanced the player builds are. DEX builds are terrible and make the game significantly more difficult and frustrating. My wife always plays a Mage in both Salt games because she likes to stand back and toss magic while I melee the bosses. I like to try different builds with every playthrough.

After completing the game with several different builds, we found mixing a strength and endurance build with a strength and magic build to be the best co-op combo for taking down Mages.

If you were a fan of Salt and Sanctuary, Salt and Sacrifice is more than worth its asking price. The combat feels more refined and the change in gameplay and systems gives it its own personality. 
HIGHLY RECOMMENDED!

I came into the game originally on PS5 two years ago. Tried it for a bit, wasn't the biggest fan and decided to come back to it.

Here I am now having played much more of it than I originally did. 

Man, I so badly wanted to like this game. 

I am a massive fan of Salt and Sanctuary, love that game so much for its style, sound design, enemy design, boss design, level design, and weapon design. 

This game feels like such a massive step back in almost all of those regards. The random spawns of bosses, hazeburnt enemies during hunts, areas that don't feel designed to have bosses in them, enemies and traps that feel like complete and utter ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ so much of the time, and late game areas that don't feel like they have any reason to be there or are unfinished.

The weapon crafting system feels so good, the player combat feels so good with the focus and rage systems of casting. Not being able to turn around while aiming is a massive downside though, lack of an attack speed indicator can turn a much anticipated weapon craft into a huge disappointment.

Souls games work because there is a level of predictability with the enemy placement. Randomly spawning enemies don't mess with the enemy placement. Enemies don't appear one moment in just the worst place imaginable with almost no time to react to it. The traps are readable, even in Sanctuary the traps were much more fun to deal with.

 Monster Hunter games work because the areas have a lot of care put into them to make sure the levels interact with the monsters you hunt. Some even have special interactions with levels. You can also pick what monster to hunt in order to grind instead of loading into a level and hoping you get what you wanted to spawn.

This game does both of those things wrong. I hate to say it feels like more of a rush job on an otherwise incredible concept for a game with the player systems all being there for a great experience. The world feels underdone. The enemies feel underdone. It's so frustrating to see a game come so close just to be held back by a very poor feeling world.

I love Ska Studios, but man this feels like a dropped ball.

For the first couple of hours, I wasn't entirely certain if I liked Salt and Sacrifice. Frustration after frustration mounted and I often felt like I was pushing my face against a wall to little progress. But then...I can't remember the exact moment I saw the cracks in the wall, worked my way into them and pulled it all tumbling down in front of me but I know it accompanied the sound of a clear thought in my head; "Wait, this isn't Dark Souls. It's Monster Hunter."

I am now on a simultaneous NG+ playthrough and a separate brand new playthrough with the completely opposite build, 43 hours deep and one achievement from 100%. After that wall was long behind me, I found Salt and Sacrifice not to be the disappointment I had thought it was at the beginning but an enjoyable game with a great amount of fun you could often find for yourself.

I dislike the moaning and groaning over reductionist takes on genre touchstones such as "Metroidvania" or "Soulslike." There's a reason we use these terms. In a limited time frame (such as a review on Steam with limited word counts) it conveys the necessary information so we can talk about more important things. If I tell you Salt and Sacrifice is a "2D soulslike" you know it has deliberate attack animations, difficult culminating boss battles, experience loss and recuperation, reusable healing flasks and so on; if I tell you it is also very much a "Monster Hunter-like" you know that you'll be hunting large enemies across the level, picking up their fallen bits and crafting new weapons and armor from them and sometimes you'll watch as they step into each other's territories and start fighting each other. The rest is the minutiae, the flavor of the game and how those pieces fit and move and the game's personal spin.

Leveling importantly has numerous branches to follow that give you the ability to wield stronger weapons and magics you harvest off fallen Mages, and you will meet that same wall I referenced at the start if you stubbornly stick to one weapon type; the game desperately implores you to experiment and switch up your items all the time. If you put all your points into rapiers and the last rapier you made was ages ago, fire damage and had a weak weapon skill you're gonna run face first into that wall when the newest Mage you have to kill takes significantly less damage and stagger from fire weapons. I'll also say, personally, I find it a waste to not use a bunch of different weapons because the sheer variety of weapon skills, what they do and how they change your fighting strategies was a ton of fun. A favorite of mine was a hammer whose strong attack created a glowing blade and used the two-hand sword moveset which changed completely how I normally fought with a bludgeon.

The story is reminiscent of normal "soulslike" storytelling, in mysterious subject matter with lots of vague terms and uncovering stuff as you go but I quite enjoyed it. It's not hard to follow (Mages bad, kill Mages), it's competently done and most importantly (and what I'd argue is more important in a game with Souls DNA) the world-building is very well thought-out and interesting. I am actually debating doing a lore-through video series. There's your surface plot and then a world bubbling under the surface that is hidden in skill and item descriptions, bestiary entries and dialogue. It's quite full and fleshed out.

Unfortunately, I cannot comment on much more since review space is limited, but I'll sum up that the music was limited but good for the tone of the areas and game and I enjoyed the design of a lot of monsters and characters. There are so many good armor sets to play fashion with and monsters that I thought looked great. An important thing I will note is that there are a great number of different factions with different multiplayer goals so it is quite obvious the devs meant to have this game be played with heavy multiplayer involvement. Refer to the aformentioned Monster Hunter comparison where multiple people should be hunting Mages but also the Soulslike comparison in that some people might invade to kill you or maybe just steal your sweet sweet Haze juice.

At the end of the day, I am extremely glad I played the game on it's terms and not the ones I wanted it to have.

I loved Salt & Sanctuary, one of my all-time favorite games, and therefore bought this game in the hope of finding a game with improvements over the previous chapter. Instead, Salt & Sacrifice keeps pretty much the same gameplay, worsening almost all other aspects. 
First, it is NOT an open-world; you have to use a teleport to access macro areas (these macro areas contain interconnected micro-areas, but the feeling is much worse than in S&Sanc).
In addition, the last areas have UNFAIR bosses (based on the pseudo-random pattern of the moveset, some attacks cannot be dodged at all). Another thing that bothered me a lot is the random spawn mechanism of common enemies during a boss "hunting" phase: it will happen sometimes that the enemy will spawn in front of your face, or during the boss fight, thus ruining your run (attempt) at random. 
It is a real shame, because Salt & Sanctuary was a diamond in the rough that simply needed polishing... The only thing it has improved in is playing with other players, nothing to say about that.

Engraçado que a primeira vez que conheci Salt and Sanctuary me falaram que era um Dark Souls 2D, de fato alguns aspectos de gameplay e a forma de contar a historia são muito similares. Antes do lançamento na steam só via analises do jogo falando que ele era inferior em comparação ao primeiro, pude comprar o jogo e testa-lo finalmente e entendi o motivo das críticas. 
 Comecei falando sobre a comparação com Dark Souls e Salt and Sanctuary pois parece que Salt and Sacrifice segue a mesma situação que Dark Souls 2, um jogo que de igual forma tinha ideias interessantes que seriam ótimos acrecemos a franquia, mas que faltou um pouco de polimento na implementação. Por exemplo:
 Dobraram o numero de chefes em comparação ao primeiro jogo que eram 19 e passou-se a ter 38 (4 novos com a atualização The traitor's Tomb), entre eles os magos, esse grupo de chefes vem junto a mecânica de caçada, onde eles podem reaparecer no mapa e você pode persegui-los para lutar com eles e conseguir mais matérias de fabricação, o problema e que situações como se teletransportar para uma região e se deparar com 4 deles no spawn invocando criaturas ou travando uma passagem em algum momento podem acontecer, essas situações caóticas podem vir a tornar sua experiencia frustante. 
 O grupo de armas aumentou(embora as pistolas se foram, F pros pistoleiros) e as interligação entre as regiões também sofreu mudanças, para alguns essa mudança pode ser ruim, boa ou indiferente, agora você escolhe a região por meio do portal em nossa safe zone, o vale do perdão. lá você se movimentara por meio dos atalhos e viajem rápida entre monumentos de descanso específicos.
 Em suma os pontos positivos e negativos que Salt and Sacrifice trazem não prejudicarão totalmente sua experiencia, desde que você não espere estar jogando um Salt and Sanctuary 2.0, mas algo novo que teve uma implementação imperfeita, mas ainda assim uma novidade bem vinda, e que se tiver um terceiro Salt and servirão de exemplo para o que pode ser acrescentado e o que pode ser melhorado.
